title: Eschatology: The Great Tribulation (cont'd)
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Lesson 10 on Eschatology_ The Great Tribulation (cont'd).txt

outline.title: Review: The Timeline of Historic Covenantal Premillennialism
subpoints: Great Tribulation, then bodily return of Christ and resurrection of believers
New heaven and earth appear at the coming of Christ; sin's curse removed but sinners present during millennium
The millennium is followed by a rebellion of unbelievers led by Satan
Then the resurrection of unbelievers for final judgment, then the eternal state

outline.title: The Olivet Discourse: Structure and Method
subpoints: Matthew 24, Mark 13, and Luke 21 all record the Olivet Discourse
Jesus is answering a three-part question from the disciples about the temple and His coming
Jesus attaches the tribulation to His physical, bodily return immediately after it
The discourse speaks of both the destruction of Jerusalem (70 AD) and the final tribulation — both as one prophetic picture

outline.title: Marks of the Tribulation: False Religion, Civil Unrest, Worldwide Evangelism
subpoints: The tribulation will be marked by false Christs and false prophets showing great signs and wonders
Nations will rise against nations; famines, pestilences, and earthquakes in various places
The gospel must first be preached to all nations before the end comes
The lawless one will be revealed — a figure or system directing nations away from Christ

outline.title: The Days of Noah: Pattern for the Tribulation
subpoints: Jesus equates the coming tribulation with the days of Noah
The flood was universal judgment on all nations for unbelief
Unbelievers ignored the building of the ark and Noah's 120-year preaching of repentance
The pattern: believers aware of tribulation; unbelievers disregard warnings and continue in wickedness

outline.title: What About the Rapture? A Preview
subpoints: The rapture is typically defined as a secret return of Christ and secret removal of believers before the tribulation
Matthew 24:40-41 ('one will be taken, the other left') is used to argue for the rapture
The ultimate rapture text is 1 Thessalonians 4:15-17 — to be addressed in the next lesson
The class will explore whether this describes a pre-tribulation removal or the post-tribulation return of Christ

summary:
This tenth Sunday school lesson in the eschatology series continues examining the biblical teaching on the Great Tribulation, with particular focus on the Olivet Discourse in Matthew 24. After reviewing the timeline of historic covenantal premillennialism — including the unique position that the new heavens and new earth arrive at Christ's physical return, allowing for sinful people to still exist during the millennium — the lesson turns to the structure and content of Jesus's teaching in Matthew 24.

The Olivet Discourse is understood to address both the destruction of Jerusalem in 70 AD (especially in Luke 21) and the final Great Tribulation — with Jesus moving between these two events as a prophet might, seeing them as a unified panorama from a prophetic distance, much as one sees mountain peaks blend together from afar. The marks of the tribulation include: false Christs and prophets performing signs and wonders; nations rising against nations with famines and earthquakes; worldwide proclamation of the gospel; and a lawless figure or system that leads people away from Christ.

Jesus's analogy of the days of Noah is examined at length: a universal flood-level judgment on the nations, preceded by years of unheeded warning and public preaching, in which God's people endure while unbelievers carry on as usual. The lesson closes with a preview of the rapture question, which will be addressed in the following lesson through 1 Thessalonians 4, and with a brief discussion of the confession's position that all infants dying in infancy go to heaven.
transcript:
## Introduction

As we wait for the equipment to turn on, would you turn in your Bible to Matthew chapter 24? That's what we're going to read from today. We'll be reading verses 15 through 28, and then continuing on. We're going to hopefully finish the subject of the tribulation as taught in the Scriptures today.

It's impossible to talk about the tribulation without also talking about the coming of Christ, because in theology it is always put in terms of the return of Christ and the resurrection. We can divide these things into categories to make them easier to study, but they really go together.

Matthew 24, starting at verse 15:

> *"Therefore when you see the 'abomination of desolation,' spoken of by Daniel the prophet, standing in the holy place (whoever reads, let him understand), then let those who are in Judea flee to the mountains. Let him who is on the housetop not go down to take anything out of his house. And let him who is in the field not go back to get his clothes. But woe to those who are pregnant and to those who are nursing babies in those days! And pray that your flight may not be in winter or on the Sabbath. For then there will be great tribulation, such as has not been since the beginning of the world until this time, no, nor ever shall be. And unless those days were shortened, no flesh would be saved; but for the elect's sake those days will be shortened. Then if anyone says to you, 'Look, here is the Christ!' or 'There!' do not believe it. For false christs and false prophets will rise and show great signs and wonders to deceive, if possible, even the elect."* (Matthew 24:15–22)

> *"Immediately after the tribulation of those days the sun will be darkened, and the moon will not give its light; the stars will fall from heaven, and the powers of the heavens will be shaken. Then the sign of the Son of Man will appear in heaven, and then all the tribes of the earth will mourn, and they will see the Son of Man coming on the clouds of heaven with power and great glory. And He will send His angels with a great sound of a trumpet, and they will gather together His elect from the four winds, from one end of heaven to the other."* (Matthew 24:29–31)

Although our Lord has given us a lot of information about the events surrounding His coming, He also told us to look for signs and be aware of things going on around the time He comes.

## Review: The Timeline of Historic Covenantal Premillennialism

Let me review the timeline we are working from. The Great Tribulation is still future. After it comes the physical, bodily return of Christ and the resurrection of believers. The new heavens and new earth happen at that time. When does the new heaven and earth happen in your scheme? In my scheme, they happen at the coming of Jesus Christ — which means that the curse of sin will be removed, but there will be sinful people on this earth during the millennium.

There are two precedents for this in the Bible. One is Adam and Eve's presence in the garden before the curse was pronounced — they existed as sinful beings in a yet-uncursed world. The other is the reverse: Jesus with His resurrection body present in a sin-cursed world. So you have a precedent for this idea.

This is followed by a thousand-year physical reign on earth — the number one thousand appears in Revelation 20. Then a rebellion of unbelievers led by Satan. Then the resurrection of unbelievers for judgment. Then the final judgment. Then the eternal state — which will look a lot like the millennium, except no sinners will be present.

## The Structure of the Olivet Discourse

Most important to understand is that Matthew 24, Mark 13, and Luke 21 are structured in response to a question asked by the disciples. Matthew 24:3:

> *"Tell us, when will these things be? And what will be the sign of Your coming, and of the end of the age?"* (Matthew 24:3)

That is a three-part question. Jesus is not trying to address everything concerning His return — He is answering the question the disciples asked, in the order they asked it.

As we read through this chapter, notice that it attaches the tribulation to the physical, bodily return of Christ. Verse 29: *"Immediately after the tribulation of those days the sun will be darkened… then the sign of the Son of Man will appear in heaven… and they will see the Son of Man coming on the clouds of heaven with power and great glory."* So Jesus places His return right after the events of the tribulation.

The disciples can be understood in two ways: as the specific men who were with Jesus, or as representative of disciples from all ages — as we see in Matthew 16 (keys to the kingdom) and Matthew 28 (the Great Commission to make disciples of all nations). So we can understand the disciples here as representing all disciples who will be present at the coming of Jesus Christ.

Bible prophecy often refers to events that happen at different times within a single prophecy — as in Isaiah 7, where a sign to Ahaz and the birth of the Messiah appear together, seen from a prophetic distance as one. The Olivet Discourse speaks both of the destruction of Jerusalem (especially clear in Luke 21) and of the ultimate Great Tribulation — with Jesus moving back and forth between these chronologically separated events. The destruction of Jerusalem is a type, a microcosm, a test case of what will happen at the return of Jesus Christ.

## Marks of the Tribulation

The tribulation will be marked by false religious claims. Matthew 24:4–5:

> *"Take heed that no one deceives you. For many will come in My name, saying, 'I am the Christ,' and will deceive many."* (Matthew 24:4–5)

And verse 24:

> *"For false christs and false prophets will rise and show great signs and wonders to deceive, if possible, even the elect."* (Matthew 24:24)

This agrees with Paul's teaching in 2 Thessalonians 2:

> *"The coming of the lawless one is according to the working of Satan, with all power, signs, and lying wonders, and with all unrighteous deception among those who perish, because they did not receive the love of the truth that they might be saved. And for this reason God will send them strong delusion, that they should believe the lie, that they all may be condemned who did not believe the truth but had pleasure in unrighteousness."* (2 Thessalonians 2:9–12)

The tribulation will also include great civil unrest. Matthew 24:7–8:

> *"For nation will rise against nation, and kingdom against kingdom. And there will be famines, pestilences, and earthquakes in various places. All these are the beginning of sorrows."* (Matthew 24:7–8)

Also, the end will not come until the entire world has been evangelized. Matthew 24:14:

> *"And this gospel of the kingdom will be preached in all the world as a witness to all the nations, and then the end will come."* (Matthew 24:14)

Being evangelized is not the same as being saved. Jesus is not claiming all the world will be saved, but that all the world will hear the gospel — and that has not yet fully happened.

The tribulation will also include a leading figure or system directing nations away from Christ. 2 Thessalonians 2 describes the lawless one whom the Lord will consume with the breath of His mouth at His coming. In 1 John, the Apostle calls this the antichrist. There is a figure or system that will lead nations away from Christ — Paul says it is currently being restrained, and when the restrainer is removed, the lawless one will be revealed.

## The Days of Noah: Pattern for the Tribulation

Jesus equates the tribulation with the days of Noah:

> *"But as the days of Noah were, so also will the coming of the Son of Man be. For as in the days before the flood, they were eating and drinking, marrying and giving in marriage, until the day that Noah entered the ark, and did not know until the flood came and took them all away."* (Matthew 24:37–39)

The one event Jesus uses to illustrate this tribulation is not a localized event — it was universal, covering the whole world. Believers (Noah's family) were aware of the coming judgment and prepared. Unbelievers disregarded all warnings — Noah preached repentance for 120 years as he built the ark, and the people disregarded it entirely. They were deeply immoral and refused to hear what God was saying.

## What About the Rapture? A Preview

The word "rapture" comes from a Latin word meaning "carrying off." In theology it usually refers to a secret return of Christ and secret removal of all believers from earth prior to the Great Tribulation. This doctrine is drawn from texts like Matthew 24:40–41:

> *"Then two men will be in the field: one will be taken and the other left. Two women will be grinding at the mill: one will be taken and the other left."* (Matthew 24:40–41)

But the ultimate passage for the rapture, according to those who believe in a secret pre-tribulation removal, is 1 Thessalonians 4:15–17. And that is where we begin next Lord's Day, Lord willing.
